revision.text:
[[ja:皇帝祭祀]] {{Redirect|Southern Suburbs|Administrative district of Datong, Shanxi Province|Nanjiao District|qq=}} [[File:TOH Another Hall 3.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Temple of Heaven|en|Temple of Heaven|qid=Q125445|lt=Temple of Heaven|1=Temple of Heaven}}
The site where the Southern Suburbs ritual was conducted during the Ming and Qing periods, now a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}.]] '''Imperial Rites''' (皇帝祭祀) refer to the state rituals conducted by the {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|qid=Q39018|lt=Emperor of China|1=Emperor}}. By performing these rites, the emperor also embodied a sacral kingship role. == Definition and Features == Imperial rites originated with the {{ill|Qin Shi Huang|lt=First Emperor of Qin|ja|秦の始皇帝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qin Shi Huang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 始皇帝|1=Qin Shi Huang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7192}}. During the {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|qid=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}}, the rites were formalized, and the distinction between the "{{ill|Son of Heaven|en|Son of Heaven|qid=Q1147432|lt=Son of Heaven|1=Son of Heaven}}" concept from the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|qid=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty|1=Zhou dynasty}} era and the "{{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|qid=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}}" established by the First Emperor was reflected in the ritual system. Imperial rites, referred to as *Jiao Temple* (郊廟) in historical records, are divided into two categories: those conducted in the ancestral {{ill|Confucian royal ancestral shrine|en|Confucian royal ancestral shrine|qid=Q10948212|lt=Temple of Ancestral Worship|1=Confucian royal ancestral shrine}}, which honored the emperor’s forebears, and those held in the suburbs of the capital, known as {{ill|Jiao Sacrifices|ja|郊祀|1=Jiao Sacrifices|lt=Jiao sacrifices (suburban worship)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jiao Sacrifices|13=User:Immanuelle/Jiao Sacrifices|qq=}}. Suburban sacrifices were further divided into the Southern Suburbs, dedicated to {{ill|Tian|en|Tian|qid=Q1364799|lt=Heaven|1=Tian}}, and the Northern Suburbs, devoted to terrestrial deities. During the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}, the system of imperial rites was codified in the *Great Tang Kaiyuan Ritual* (*Dà Táng Kāiyuán Lǐ*), which became a model for state ceremonial texts in later dynasties, such as the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}}. The *Kaiyuan Ritual* categorized state ceremonies into three scales: major, intermediate, and minor. Imperial rites were practiced not only by Han Chinese dynasties but also by non-Han regimes such as the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Northern Dynasties|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, {{ill|Liao dynasty|en|Liao dynasty|qid=Q4958|lt=Liao dynasty|1=Liao dynasty}}, {{ill|Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|en|Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|qid=Q5066|lt=Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|1=Jin dynasty (1115–1234)}}, {{ill|Yuan dynasty|en|Yuan dynasty|qid=Q7313|lt=Yuan dynasty|1=Yuan dynasty}}, and {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|qid=Q8733|lt=Qing dynasty|1=Qing dynasty}}. Although there were modifications, these rituals continued in tandem with the imperial system. Imperial rites also influenced {{ill|Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|en|Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|qid=Q11453226|lt=Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace|1=Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}}, including the {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|qid=Q11501518|lt=Niiname-sai|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}. == Historical Development == The origins of imperial rites are debated, but a common explanation combines the natural deity worship (*She Ji*) of southern states like {{ill|Chu (state)|ja|楚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chu (state)|1=Chu (state)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q398797}} and {{ill|Shu (state)|ja|蜀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shu (state)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蜀漢|1=Shu Han|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q320925}} with the ancestor worship (*Zong Miao*) of the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|qid=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty|1=Zhou dynasty}}. Added to this was the suburban sacrifice to Heaven (*Jiao Tian*), believed to have been initiated by the First Emperor of Qin. The rites included the Southern Suburbs (Heaven), the Northern Suburbs (earth deities), and the ancestral temples (ancestors), collectively referred to as *Jiao Temple*. This synthesis represented the unification of southern {{ill|Yangtze|en|Yangtze|qid=Q5413|lt=Yangtze River|1=Yangtze}} and northern {{ill|Yellow River|en|Yellow River|qid=Q7355|lt=Yellow River|1=Yellow River}} ritual traditions under the {{ill|Qin (state)|en|Qin (state)|qid=Q34756|lt=Qin dynasty|1=Qin (state)}}] and {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|qid=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}}] empires. === Evolution of the Jiao Sacrifices === Under the {{ill|Western Han dynasty|ja|前漢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western Han dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Western Han|qid=Q1072949}}, sacrifices to Heaven occurred south of the capital during the {{ill|Winter solstice|en|Winter solstice|qid=Q207932|lt=Winter Solstice|1=Winter solstice}}, while sacrifices to the earth took place in the north during the {{ill|Summer solstice|en|Summer solstice|qid=Q7603898|lt=Summer Solstice|1=Summer solstice}}. These rituals were called Southern Suburbs and Northern Suburbs, respectively. Over time, emphasis shifted to the Southern Suburbs, and by the {{ill|Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|en|Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|qid=Q7209|lt=Later Han|1=Han dynasty}}{{citation needed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Han dynasty|qq=}}, only a single Southern Suburbs sacrifice was conducted annually during the {{ill|Japanese New Year|en|Japanese New Year|qid=Q701398|lt=Japanese New Year|1=Japanese New Year}}. === Changes in Ancestral Temple Rituals === Ancestral temples (*Zong Miao*) housed the spirit tablets of ancestors and were central to the worship of royal lineages from the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|en|Spring and Autumn period|qid=Q185047|lt=Spring and Autumn period|1=Spring and Autumn period}}. During the {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|qid=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}}, {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}} became the state ideology, which regulated the number of ancestors enshrined based on Rank. The emperor was permitted to worship up to seven generations, centered on the founder (*Taizu*) of the dynasty, whose shrine was called the *Ta Temple* (*Ta Miao*). === Coronation Ceremonies === Ancestral temple rituals were closely linked to imperial coronations. In the {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|qid=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}}, new emperors performed rites before the deceased emperor's coffin (*Xian Qian Jiwei*). However, over time, coronation rites in open suburban spaces, visible to the public, gained prominence. === Connection to Japanese Coronation Rites === Japan's imperial coronation rite, the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|qid=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}}, includes offerings to the {{ill|Big Dipper|en|Big Dipper|qid=Q10460|lt=Big Dipper|1=Big Dipper}} and {{ill|Southern Dipper|ja|南斗六星|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Dipper|1=Milk Dipper|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10907953}}, reflecting parallels to Chinese imperial coronations. However, the Japanese emperor’s role as both worshipper and object of worship marks a distinction from the Chinese emperor, who acted solely as a mediator. == See Also == *{{ill|Sacrifice to Heaven|en|Sacrifice to Heaven|qid=Q11591092|lt=Sacrifice to Heaven|1=Sacrifice to Heaven}} == References == {{No footnotes|date=2018th year 11月|section=1|qq=}} *{{ill|Mizoguchi Yuzo|ja|溝口雄三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizoguchi Yuzo|1=Yūzō Mizoguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11564487}} et al., ''Dictionary of Chinese Thought and Culture,'' Tokyo University Press, 2001. *{{ill|Kaneko Shuichi|ja|金子修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaneko Shuichi|1=Shūichi Kaneko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11646711}}, ''Ancient China and Imperial Rites,'' Kyuko Shoin, 2001. *{{ill|Yoshino Hiroko|ja|吉野裕子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Hiroko|1=Hiroko Yoshino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11414516}}, ''The Daijosai: Structure of the Emperor's Coronation,'' Kobundo, 1987. {{translated page|ja|皇帝祭祀|version=70595941|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:皇帝祭祀]] {{wikidata link|Q11581058}} [[Category:Ancient Chinese institutions]] [[Category:History of Chinese thought]]
